text: ACT IV
THE SHADOWS
A sincere self-critique: what sovereignty can betray
"Freedom without responsibility is not liberation. It is another form of abandonment." — David Berthelotte
Preliminary note

The first three acts of this book spoke of promises. They described a world where families take back control of their energy, their food, their communication. They painted images of liberation, of dignity reclaimed, of resilience built stone by stone. Those promises are real. I believe them. But a book that presents only the promises of an idea is not an honest book — it is a brochure. And you deserve better than a brochure.

This act is the hardest to write. Not because the dark scenarios are rare or hypothetical, but precisely because they are not. Every system of freedom that humanity has invented — the free press, the right to silence, the secrecy of correspondence, political asylum — has been used both to protect the innocent and to shelter the guilty. The distributed sovereignty this book proposes will be no exception. It would be a dangerous naivety to believe otherwise.

I am therefore going to do something that few authors of visionary books allow themselves: I will inhabit the darkest scenarios, not merely list them. I will enter the homes where sovereignty becomes a cage. The apartments where encryption protects violence. The servers where reputations are built like movie sets. And I will tell you, honestly, what the proposed safeguards can and cannot do. Because my credibility — and more importantly, the credibility of the project itself — depends on that honesty.

Sovereignty is a right, not a guarantee of virtue. Just as freedom of expression protects just speech and hateful speech alike. Just as the right to silence protects the innocent and the guilty alike. A society that strips fundamental rights from all to prevent the abuses of a few is not a safe society — it is a society that has chosen the cage over the risk of living free. But a society that grants freedom without demanding responsibility is not a free society either — it is an anarchy that devours its own children.
Chapter 1: The home as sanctuary, the home as prison
"The walls that protect us from the world are sometimes the very walls that imprison us within." — anonymous

Laval, a night in October. From the outside, nothing unusual. A house like any other in a neighborhood like any other — mowed lawn, car in the driveway, dimmed lights behind the curtains. On the family's local network, communications are end-to-end encrypted. The administrator of the digital home — the father — is the only one holding the keys. The neighbours know nothing. The colleagues know nothing. Child protective services know nothing. The children, however, know everything.

Let us begin with the most difficult contradiction, the one I would have preferred not to name so directly. The sovereign home — this vision of the family sanctuary where each family manages its own data, its own communications, its own digital space — rests on a fundamental presupposition: that the home is a space of trust. That the people living in it all share an interest in its protection. That the sovereignty of the family unit reflects the sovereignty of each of its members.

This presupposition is false for one in four families in Canada. According to the most recent Statistics Canada data, approximately 44% of Canadian women have reported experiencing physical or sexual violence since the age of sixteen. Domestic violence accounts for a significant proportion of all reported violent crimes. And these figures only count reported cases — yet in a home where communications are encrypted and controlled by the abuser, the likelihood of a case being reported decreases. This is not a theoretical hypothesis. It is a documented, repeated, tragic reality.
The structural paradox

Here is the crux of the problem, in its barest form: the same architecture that gives the Tremblay family the freedom to hold their Sunday evening council without Hydro-Québec, Meta, or the provincial government being able to listen — that same architecture gives a violent father the ability to totally control the communication environment of his victims. He can read their messages. He can monitor their calls. He can block outside contacts. He can create a perfectly sealed environment of isolation, and the system we built to liberate families becomes the instrument of some families' imprisonment.

This is not an implementation detail. It is a structural contradiction at the very heart of the model. The absolute sovereignty of the home is incompatible with the protection of the most vulnerable members inside the home. These two values — family sovereignty and the protection of children and women who are victims of violence — collide head-on in certain contexts. It is impossible to maximize both simultaneously. Any system designer who claims otherwise is lying to you, even with the best intentions in the world.
Digital aggression as an extension of physical aggression

Security researchers have a term for this phenomenon: "technological coercive control." In abusive situations, the abusive partner systematically uses technology as a tool of control. He installs surveillance software on phones. He monitors social media accounts. He controls access to money through banking apps. In a poorly designed sovereign home, the administrator of the domestic network would have access to even more powerful tools — and ones that are legitimate from the system's own perspective.

The victim, meanwhile, finds herself in a situation reminiscent of the worst forms of isolation documented in the literature on cults and totalitarian regimes. Her communication space is entirely monitored by her abuser. Her attempts to contact the outside world can be blocked or observed. And unlike pre-digital situations where the victim could at least make a phone call from a post office or a neighbour's house, the sovereign home risks wrapping every act of communication in a layer of cryptography that the abuser controls.
The proposed safeguards

Here is what the Gen_Home design can do to mitigate this problem, and here is honestly what it cannot do. The first proposed measure is what we call an individual emergency channel: every member of the household — including children above a certain age — has a direct communication channel to the outside that does not pass through the home administrator. This channel is encrypted separately, with a key that only the user holds. The home administrator cannot read it, cannot block it, and cannot know whether it has been used.

The second measure is an emergency alert mechanism that leaves no trace in the home's system logs. A simple gesture — a long press on a physical button or a sequence of gestures defined during setup — sends an encrypted alert directly to child protective services or to domestic violence hotlines. The alert contains an approximate geolocation and a pre-configured situation code. The abuser does not see the alert in the logs. The system does not mention its activation. From the outside, nothing has changed.

Individual emergency channel: implemented as an isolated application in a memory space separate from the main system, with encryption keys stored on a secure element (SE) of the personal device rather than on the home server. The network administrator can see that encrypted traffic left the network, but can neither decrypt the content nor identify the sending application. The physical alert mechanism uses a secondary radio channel (Bluetooth LE or backup 4G/LTE) distinct from the domestic Wi-Fi network, making network blocking ineffective.

HONEST LIMITATION: These mechanisms assume that the victim has access to a personal device she controls. In advanced coercive control situations, the abuser may have confiscated or physically monitored all devices. No software solution protects a person whose physical movements are entirely controlled. Technology can shorten the path to help — it cannot replace the human network of loved ones, neighbours, and professionals who see, who recognize, and who act. The most dangerous mistake would be to believe that our system solves this problem. It can mitigate it. It does not solve it.
The fundamental political question

There is a philosophical and political question that arises here with particular acuity: should we deny all families digital sovereignty because some families are abusive? Liberal democracy has already answered this question in other domains. We do not ban kitchen knives because some people use them as weapons. We do not ban cars because some drivers cause fatal accidents. We do not ban the right to silence because some criminals abuse it.

The standard democratic answer is: no, we do not deprive all citizens of a fundamental right to prevent the abuses of a minority. We target the specific abuses. We strengthen intervention resources. We create accessible reporting mechanisms. We train professionals to recognize the signs of abuse even when communications are encrypted. We rely on human networks — doctors, teachers, social workers — who see children and adults outside the home.

This is not the perfect answer. Human networks have their own blind spots. Professionals lack training and resources. Some abuses will not be detected, and vulnerable people will pay that price with their safety. This truth is painful. It is also true in the current world, with or without sovereign homes. The relevant question is not: is the sovereign world perfect? The question is: is the sovereign world better than the current world, for the majority of vulnerable people? My answer is: probably, if the safeguards are designed seriously. But "probably" is not "certainly," and that nuance matters.

If you or someone you know lives in a home where technology is used as a tool of control, resources exist now, without waiting for any sovereign architecture: SOS Violence Conjugale at 1-800-363-9010, available 24/7. The technology this book describes does not protect you yet. The humans behind those lines can.
Chapter 2: The criminal fortress

"The same press that printed the Encyclopédie printed the pamphlets of hate. The same freedom that protects Voltaire protects his enemies. That is the price of freedom of expression." — adapted from Condorcet

An apartment in a Montreal tower. Fourteenth floor, view of the St. Lawrence. Clean, austere. The kitchen is fitted with high-end appliances. On the table, two laptops. The communication system used by the two men sitting across from each other is identical, byte for byte, to the one installed in the Laval house from the previous chapter. They are discussing supply routes for fentanyl. They are discussing prices. The encryption is the same. The signal is the same. The content could not be more different.

Here is a mathematical truth that advocates of digital sovereignty must accept without equivocation: inviolable communications mean that criminals will use them. This is not a possible risk. It is a certainty. In today's world, sophisticated criminal organizations already use Signal, Wickr, customised Tor networks, and dedicated phones bought with cash. They use servers based in non-cooperative jurisdictions. They have entire teams dedicated to operational security.

The question is therefore not: will criminals use sovereign communication systems? They already do. The question is: does the mass adoption of digital sovereignty by the general population make investigators' work harder? The honest answer is: yes, in some cases, and no in others. Yes, because the haystack is larger when everyone encrypts. No, because the criminals were already in the haystack, and the majority of successful investigations today do not primarily rely on communication interception but on human informants, operational mistakes, and traditional physical surveillance.
The history of every freedom technology

The printing press made possible the Protestant Reformation, the spread of the Enlightenment, the American Constitution — and anti-Semitic pamphlets, mass propaganda, homemade bomb manuals. The postal service allowed families separated by oceans to maintain a human bond — and it allowed blackmailers to send their demands anonymously. The telephone revolutionised the coordination of businesses and families — and it created the telephone harassment industry. The internet gave millions of people access to the entirety of human knowledge — and it created global markets for every form of criminality imaginable.

This pattern is no coincidence. It reflects a deep reality about the nature of communication and freedom tools: they amplify what humans already do, for better and for worse. They do not invent new moral categories — they change the scale and accessibility of categories that already existed. Cruelty existed before the internet. Paedophilia existed before encrypted networks. Drug trafficking existed before Signal. What these technologies do is shift the balance between different actors: some activities become easier, others more difficult.
The cost calculation

Let us pose the question directly, without euphemism. The price of universal communications sovereignty — if granted to everyone — is that certain crimes will be easier to commit and harder to detect. Drug traffickers will operate more efficiently. Fraudsters will coordinate their activities with less risk of interception. Criminal organizations will consolidate their logistics operations. This is not a hypothesis — it is the direction the tool is already taking, even in its current incomplete state.

On the other side of the equation: the price of universal surveillance. The billions of people whose communications are currently accessible to repressive governments, to companies that monetise their intimate conversations, to hackers who exploit their health data, to employers who scrutinise their union activities. The Belarusian dissident whose identity is revealed to a regime that will make her disappear. The victim of domestic violence whose messages to her sister are read by her abuser through a "family safety" app. The journalist whose sources are identified through metadata analysis.

There is no clean third option. Proposals for "government backdoors" — special access for authorities into encryption systems — have been exhaustively analyzed by cryptographers. The conclusion is unequivocal: a backdoor for the good government is a backdoor for all governments and for all criminals sophisticated enough to find it. Technically, strong cryptography is universal or it does not exist. You cannot build a vault that opens only for the good guys.

THE UNCOMFORTABLE TRUTH: My project holds that every family should have access to inviolable communications. This position implies — logically, inevitably — that criminal families will also have access to inviolable communications. I cannot dodge this. I can point out that this situation already exists for well-funded criminal organizations. I can argue that the benefits for the majority outweigh the costs of minority abuses. But if you honestly ask me: "Does your system make certain forms of criminality easier?" My honest answer is: yes, probably. This cost exists. It must be weighed, not ignored.
The answer that is not "surveil everything"

If universal surveillance is ineffective against organized criminals (who already encrypt) and destructive for ordinary citizens, and if universal freedom has a real criminal cost — what is the way forward? The answer I propose is not a technical solution. It is a reaffirmation of a political priority. Democratic societies have always operated with a certain sphere of private freedom that permits certain abuses, because they judged that freedom was worth that price. This decision was not made once and for all — it is constantly renegotiated.

Effective police work has never primarily relied on mass surveillance of communications. It relies on community trust, informants, fieldwork, financial analysis, physical surveillance targeted at specific individuals or groups with judicial warrants. These methods work in an encrypted world. They require more human resources and fewer technological resources. They require police forces that have the trust of communities — which, in certain Canadian contexts, is precisely what is lacking.

The real public policy question is not "surveillance or freedom" — it is "which public investments yield the best results in terms of security while respecting fundamental rights?" This question deserves an open democratic debate, not a technical decision made behind closed doors by engineers, legislators, or authors of visionary books.

Are you prepared to accept that your right to private communication also protects the right to private communication of people you disapprove of? Or do you believe your privacy should be protected, but not theirs? If so, who gets to draw the line — and who ensures that you are not placed on the other side of that line one day?
Chapter 3: The poisoned reputation
"Trust is built over years and destroyed in seconds. In a distributed system, it can also be built in seconds — if you know how." — David Berthelotte

Quebec City, an apartment in the Old Port. Two years on the UBLinx network. Two years of contributions: neighbour validations, resource sharing, community garden coordination. Five-star ratings, an impeccable track record. Other network members trust them for exchanges involving thousands of dollars' worth of solar equipment. All of it is fabricated. The validations come from secondary accounts they control themselves. The community contributions are real — but they are only the veneer on a scam carefully planned from the very beginning. They are waiting for trust to run deep enough. When the moment comes, they will vanish with the equipment.

Decentralized reputation is one of the most powerful and most fragile concepts in the entire system this book proposes. The principle is elegant: rather than relying on a central institution to certify the reliability of a person or an organization, the network itself accumulates evidence of behavior on an immutable blockchain. Every interaction leaves a trace. Every validation is recorded. Nobody can erase their past. Reputation becomes a verifiable, portable social asset, resistant to manipulation.

In theory. The reality of decentralized reputation systems — as they exist today on various blockchains and platforms — is considerably more nuanced. Several security research teams have documented what the field calls "slow reputation-building attacks": malicious actors who invest real time and resources to build a solid reputation, then exploit it once it is sufficiently valued. This is exactly what the opening scene describes. And it is far harder to detect than a simple quick scam.
The Sybil attack and its variants

The most fundamental attack against any decentralized reputation system bears a biblical-sounding name: the Sybil attack, named after the clinical case of a woman diagnosed with dissociative identity disorder. In a Sybil attack, a single actor creates numerous distinct identities and uses them to mutually validate one another, artificially inflate their reputation, and dominate governance or voting mechanisms. This is the problem that every decentralized reputation system must solve before it can be useful.

The UBLinx network, as described in the previous acts, uses several layers of defense against this attack. The first is physical identity anchoring: a reputation is tied to an identity verified through a process that requires physical presence in at least one known location — a neighborhood, a postal code, a geographically consistent IP address over an extended period. The second layer is cross-validation with real cost: to validate someone, you stake a fraction of your own reputation. If the person you validated turns out to be fraudulent, you incur an automatic penalty. This cost discourages free validations of fictitious identities.

Resistance to Sybil attacks in distributed networks is an active area of research. Promising approaches include: Proof of Personhood using synchronised cryptographic ceremonies, trust graphs with cluster analysis (detecting islands of mutually validated identities), and "social collateral" systems where people physically located in different places must validate an identity for it to be considered robust. None of these approaches is perfect against a determined and well-funded adversary.

The third defense is the immutable permanence of transactions on the blockchain. A fraudster who abandons their compromised identity cannot erase their actions. The history remains accessible in perpetuity. This is precisely why slow reputation scams are risky for their perpetrators — if a pattern emerges later, all their past transactions can be re-examined. The distributed social body has a perfect memory. It may take time to recognize a toxic element, but it never forgets one.
The real limits of these defenses

Here is where I must be honest with you: these defenses are effective against amateur fraudsters. They are markedly less effective against sophisticated and patient actors. A professional criminal organization with sufficient resources can invest two years building "real" reputations — by genuinely conducting honest transactions, genuinely contributing to community gardens, genuinely validating neighbours with real smartphones at real locations. The cost of this investment can be considerable, but not impossible if the expected payoff of the final scam is large enough.

CRITICAL LIMITATION: The most dangerous fraudsters are not those who get caught. They are those who never get caught. No reputation system — decentralized, centralized, human or algorithmic — is impervious to a sufficiently sophisticated and patient actor. Centralized systems like Amazon and Yelp are massively manipulated by industrial fake review farms. The distributed systems proposed here have a higher sophistication threshold for manipulation, but a lower threshold for the persistence of damage once fraud is committed. There is no absolute superiority — there are different risk profiles.
The deeper cultural challenge

There is a limitation that is not technical, and that may be the most important of all. Decentralized reputation systems require a profound shift in how people understand trust. We have been culturally programmed, for centuries, to trust institutions: banks, courts, professional certification bodies, state apparatuses. These institutions have considerable flaws — they discriminate, they become corrupt, they systematically fail marginalised people. But they have also created familiar recourse mechanisms. When a bank cheats you, there is a process. When a contract is violated, there are courts.

In a distributed reputation network, who arbitrates complex disputes? Who decides that a fraud is a fraud? Who can compel restitution? Decentralized governance mechanisms — DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations), reputation-weighted voting systems — exist and function for certain types of simple disputes. But they struggle with real human complexity: good-faith disputes, cultural misunderstandings, situations of vulnerability where one party had less power than the other even though both formally consented.

This is not a reason to abandon decentralized reputation systems. It is a reason to design them with humility, recognising that they work better as a complement to existing institutions than as a total replacement for them. At least in the early phases of their deployment. Trust in networks is built over generations, not over technology cycles.

Do you trust your neighbours? Your neighbours in the street you know by sight? Your neighbours in the city you have never met? The answers to these questions define the natural limits of distributed reputation systems — and suggest that their deployment should begin with circles where trust already exists, before expanding.
Chapter 4: The darkest blind spot
"Some truths are hard to write. They must be written anyway." — David Berthelotte

A cybersecurity researcher, in a Montreal office, reads the Gen_Home architecture description for the first time. She has spent ten years of her career tracking child exploitation networks. Her first reaction, upon reading the section on end-to-end encryption and sovereign home administration, is not enthusiasm. It is a familiar nausea. She knows exactly how this architecture will be used by certain people. She has seen enough case files to know.

There are scenarios we prefer not to name. Not because they are impossible — but because naming them seems to make them more real, more accessible, and because the author who names them in the positive context of a visionary book fears being associated with promoting them. I understand that fear. I reject it nonetheless. The reason is simple: the scenario I am about to describe is the most important in this entire act. It is the one where digital sovereignty risks betraying its own values most fundamentally. If I cannot write about it honestly, I have no right to write this book.

The "think of the children" argument is one of the most instrumentalised in the recent history of digital policy. It has been used to justify mass surveillance laws whose real targets were political. It has been used to ban encryption tools whose users were primarily journalists and dissidents. It has been used to legitimise internet control regimes that ended up serving authoritarian governments. Distrust of this argument is therefore rational.

But sometimes this argument is invoked in good faith, by people who have seen with their own eyes what certain people do to children under the cover of privacy. And in those cases, automatic distrust of the argument is a moral error. Both things are true simultaneously: "Think of the children" is frequently manipulated by actors whose real objectives are authoritarian, AND the protection of children from exploitation is an absolute value that cannot be sacrificed on the altar of ideological consistency.
The contradiction I cannot resolve

Here is the state of the question as precisely as I can formulate it. The exploitation of children through digital networks is a real, documented crime, with millions of victims. The specialized police forces that investigate these crimes rely partly on detection tools within digital communications — notably automatic systems that analyze transmitted images and identify them as prohibited material before they are even encrypted. These systems, imperfect and controversial, have contributed to real investigations.

An end-to-end encrypted communication architecture, where the home administrator controls the keys, makes these detection systems impossible to deploy without the administrator's cooperation. And an administrator who uses the home to abuse children will not cooperate. This limitation is real. It cannot be circumvented by a technical trick within the framework of a genuinely encrypted system. It is a fundamental architectural choice with real moral consequences.
The proposed measures and their honesty

There are cryptographic approaches called "zero-knowledge proofs" that theoretically allow verification of whether content matches a problematic pattern without revealing the content itself. In simple terms: a system can say "this file matches a known hash of child exploitation material" without ever seeing the file. This approach has been proposed and partially implemented in various industry initiatives. It represents a real path forward.

Detection systems based on cryptographic hashes (such as Microsoft's PhotoDNA, or equivalent systems) compare the digital fingerprints of files against a database of known material without analysing the content. Variants using "fuzzy hashes" can detect slightly modified versions. Work in applied cryptography explores zero-knowledge proofs that would allow this detection without the content ever being transmitted in cleartext to a third party. These systems are real and functional for cases of known material. They do not detect new material.

There are also community mechanisms: reporting systems where network members can alert designated authorities without the reporting chain being visible to the home administrator. And architectural invariants — constraints built into the deepest layers of the system that make certain actions physically impossible regardless of the administrator's behavior, such as the automatic transmission of certain patterns to external authorities.

THE HARDEST TRUTH IN THIS BOOK: These measures are imperfect, circumventable, and themselves carry risks of abuse. A system for detecting prohibited material is also a system for surveilling communications — the boundary between the two is technical and therefore crossable. A community reporting mechanism can be hijacked to harass minority members or families that deviate from dominant cultural norms. There is no technical solution to the problem of child exploitation that does not simultaneously create infrastructure potentially usable for other forms of control. This claim is verifiable. It is also profoundly uncomfortable. It does not disappear because it is uncomfortable.

Yoshua Bengio, one of the founding fathers of deep learning and one of the world's most respected artificial intelligence researchers, has devoted a significant portion of his recent years to warning about the use of AI tools against the most vulnerable. His point is not that AI is bad — it is that the tools we build with the best intentions can be turned against those we meant to protect. The same communication architecture we are building to liberate families can be weaponised against the most vulnerable members of those families.

This reality does not lead me to abandon the project. It leads me to demand that the architectural invariants — the ethical constraints encoded in the deepest layers of the system, described in the first book of this series — explicitly and unconditionally include the protection of children as a non-negotiable principle. Not as a configuration option. Not as a best-practice recommendation. As a physical limit of what is possible. The first book of this series describes how certain values can be encoded into the very matter of a system, making their violation architecturally impossible. The protection of children must be part of this.

I know this position is incomplete. I know that an architectural limit is not enough against determined adversaries. I know that detecting new material escapes every known hash system. I know that the most solid invariant can be circumvented with enough determination and resources. What I can assert is that the protection of children is the line I am not prepared to cross in the name of ideological consistency. If an architecture cannot be designed so as not to facilitate the exploitation of children, then that architecture does not deserve to be built.

If you were an engineer and were asked to design a sovereign communication system, how would you position the automatic detection of child exploitation material relative to the privacy of millions of innocent users? There is no obvious right answer. But the absence of an obvious right answer does not exempt us from the obligation to search for one.
Chapter 5: Efficient centralization versus distributed sovereignty
"The best is the enemy of the good. The best enemy of freedom is sometimes efficiency." — David Berthelotte

An emergency room in Sherbrooke. Eleven o'clock at night. A sixty-eight-year-old woman arrives unconscious, brought in by paramedics who found her in her kitchen. She has no identification on her. In the current centralized health system, her medical file is accessible in ninety seconds: her allergies, her medications, her cardiac history, her blood type. In a system where every family sovereignly manages its own health data space, her file is locked behind keys that her family, at two in the morning, has not yet answered the phone to provide. The emergency physician makes decisions with less information. Perhaps the right decisions. Perhaps not.

This is the moment where I must defend the adversary of my own position. Not out of rhetorical honesty — but because centralization has arguments that are true, important, and often neglected in discussions that pit freedom against control. Advocates of decentralization tend to speak of the abuses of centralization without speaking of its real successes, its daily miracles, what it enables that decentralization does not easily enable.
What centralization has truly accomplished

The global distribution of polio vaccines required centralized coordination of a precision and scale that no decentralized network could have achieved at the same speed. The World Health Organization, despite all its bureaucratic flaws, coordinated campaigns that saved tens of millions of lives. Modern weather forecasting — which saves thousands of lives every year by enabling evacuation before hurricanes — relies on centralized data collection systems, shared supercomputers, and coordinated dissemination. Air traffic control, which allows thousands of aircraft to share the same airspace without collision, is a masterpiece of centralized coordination.

These examples are not anecdotal. They represent entire classes of problems where centralization is not merely convenient — it is fundamentally necessary. Network externalities, common goods that require global coordination, emergencies that do not allow for distributed deliberation — these situations exist and they are real. A decentralising ideologue who refuses to admit this is as intellectually dishonest as a centralising ideologue who refuses to admit the abuses of surveillance.

The COVID-19 pandemic provided a full-scale test of this tension. Countries with centralized health systems and well-coordinated digital registries had, on average, better capacity for contact tracing and vaccine distribution. This is not a political claim — it is an empirical observation documented in the epidemiological literature. The centralization of health data has costs in terms of privacy and surveillance risk. It also has measurable benefits in terms of public health.
The case against centralization: the reasons you already know

But you already know the other side. You have read the reports on massive data breaches — billions of health records exposed in hospital hacks, tens of millions of social security numbers stolen in attacks on government agencies. You have read the analyses on algorithmic discrimination in centralized credit, insurance, and criminal justice systems — systems that reproduce and amplify human biases at an industrial scale. You have followed the Snowden revelations on mass surveillance of communications, including those of ordinary citizens with no connection whatsoever to criminal activity.

These abuses are not accidents. They are the predictable consequences of centralising informational power. When you concentrate data on millions of people in a single location, you create an extraordinarily valuable asset — for the legitimate administrators of that system, and also for anyone who wishes to exploit, corrupt, or seize that system. Centralization creates targets. And targets are attacked.

THE DILEMMA THAT HAS NO CLEAN RESOLUTION: Centralization saves lives in emergency situations and enables collective goods that are otherwise impossible. It also creates control infrastructures that have been and will be used against vulnerable populations. Decentralization protects privacy and individual autonomy. It also makes certain collective goods more difficult or impossible to produce. This is not a technical problem to solve. It is a permanent tension of values to manage. Anyone who tells you they have "solved" this tension — toward centralization OR toward decentralization — is oversimplifying a problem that does not simplify.
The hybrid answer and its honest limits

There is an intellectually defensible position between total centralization and absolute decentralization. It looks something like this: digital sovereignty is the default — every individual and every family controls their data and communications. But there are chosen, temporary, auditable, and revocable exceptions to this default. A person can choose to share their health data with a medical registry, can revoke that sharing at any time, and can verify when their data has been accessed and by whom. In a verifiable medical emergency, emergency access can be requested by a physician and approved by an automatic mechanism with notification to next of kin.

This is not a backdoor. A backdoor is secret access designed to be used without the data owner's knowledge. What I am describing is a front door that you open yourself, deliberately, under conditions you define. The difference is not semantic — it is fundamental. A backdoor is designed to be used without consent. An emergency front door is designed to be used with prior consent, under explicit conditions.

The technical distinction exists. But we must be honest about its practical fragility. An "auditable emergency door" becomes a backdoor the moment the actors who can demand its opening have more power than the data owner. A government that can declare a "national emergency" at will can turn any conditional access into permanent access. A company powerful enough can draft terms of service that make your "voluntary choice" a choice between accepting their conditions or not having access to essential services. The legal mechanisms protecting these doors are at least as important as the technical mechanisms.

In other words: the hybrid answer is technically possible and socially desirable. But its durability does not depend on technology — it depends on the strength of the democratic institutions that protect individual rights against abuses of power. A perfectly designed architecture does not survive a regime that decides to exploit it. And regimes change.

If you could design the ideal data-sharing system — the one you yourself would use — what portion of your personal data would you share, with whom, under what conditions, and with what guarantees of revocability? And if the current government were replaced tomorrow by a government you do not trust, would you change your answer?
Chapter 6: The safeguards and their limits

"There is no cage that protects without imprisoning. There is no freedom that liberates without exposing. The choice is not between safety and risk. It is between different types of risks, for different people." — David Berthelotte

We have traversed five difficult chapters. We have lived in homes where sovereignty becomes a prison. We have followed criminal conversations encrypted with the same architecture as ordinary family conversations. We have watched reputations carefully constructed like movie sets. We have looked squarely at the scenario no one wants to name. And we have honestly confronted the real cases where centralization produces miracles that decentralization cannot replicate.

It is now time to take stock. Not to conclude that everything is fine and that the safeguards fix everything — they do not. Not to conclude that the project is indefensible — I do not believe it is. But to be precise about what each protective mechanism can and cannot do, and about the conditions that would make them more or less effective.
Inventory of safeguards and their real limits
1. Individual emergency channel (protection against domestic abuse)

What it does: gives every member of the household a direct communication channel to the outside, not monitorable by the domestic network administrator. What it does not do: protect a person whose devices are all physically controlled by an abuser. Cannot function if the victim does not have a moment of solitude to use it. Does not protect against physical surveillance (looking over the shoulder). Its abuse potential: a child can use it to lie to their parents in a non-emergency situation, creating false reports that overwhelm intervention resources.
2. Zero-knowledge proofs (detection without surveillance)

What it does: theoretically allows detection of whether a file matches a known problematic pattern without revealing the file's content. What it does not do: detect new content that does not match any known hash. Cannot be deployed without creating a comparison infrastructure that can potentially be extended to other types of content. Its abuse potential: the same infrastructure that detects prohibited images can be used to detect political dissent if the databases of "problematic content" are expanded without democratic oversight.
3. Permanent blockchain reputation (fraud resistance)

What it does: makes it impossible to erase one's behavioral history, deterring quick scams by making the scammer's identity traceable in perpetuity. What it does not do: prevent slow reputation-building fraud by patient, well-funded actors. Does not resolve complex disputes or situations where both parties acted in good faith but with incompatible expectations. Its abuse potential: the permanence of history can be used to stigmatise people who made past mistakes and can never start over.
4. Architectural invariants (non-circumventable ethical constraints)

What it does: encodes certain behaviors as physically impossible within the system rather than merely prohibited by software rules. What it does not do: resist an adversary who modifies the hardware or creates an alternative unconstrained implementation. Cannot cover every possible ethical case — the list of invariants reflects the values of the designers at the time of design, not all possible values in all future cultural contexts. Its abuse potential: invariants designed with good intentions can have unforeseen harmful effects on populations whose legitimate practices differ from those of the designers.
5. Community accountability mechanisms

What it does: allows the distributed network to identify and exclude problematic behaviors through a process of collective governance rather than central control. What it does not do: protect minority members against majorities that reject them for cultural, ethnic, or ideological reasons rather than actual behavioral ones. Cannot function quickly in emergency situations. Its abuse potential: community governance can reproduce and amplify the prejudices of the majority, targeting individuals who differ from the group's norm.
6. Integrated mandatory reporting

What it does: creates automatic channels to the relevant authorities for certain categories of behavior without the reporting being detectable or blockable by the home administrator. What it does not do: function in homes where the problematic behavior leaves no digital trace. Cannot replace the human judgement of professionals trained to recognize the signs of abuse. Its abuse potential: automated reporting systems can generate false positives that expose innocent families to intrusive investigations.
7. Auditable temporary access protocols

What it does: allows chosen, revocable exceptions to sovereignty-by-default for medical emergencies or crisis coordination situations. What it does not do: resist a government that decides its permanent state of emergency justifies the permanent activation of these protocols. Does not guarantee that the revocation mechanism will be respected by sufficiently powerful actors. Its abuse potential: every exception creates a precedent and an infrastructure that bad-faith actors may seek to expand.
The fundamental thesis of this act

Freedom without responsibility is anarchy. Anarchy devours its own children — the most vulnerable first, always. But responsibility without freedom is oppression. Oppression also devours its own children — the most different first, always. We do not have to choose between two forms of destruction. We have to build systems that maximize freedom for the majority while effectively protecting the most vulnerable against the abuses of those closest to them.

This project — distributed digital sovereignty, homes that control their own space, communities that build their own trust networks — is not an ideology. It is not a pure ideal to be applied without regard for consequences. It is a wager. A wager that the benefits for the majority outweigh the costs of minority abuses. A wager that the protective mechanisms, imperfect but real, sufficiently mitigate the risks for the balance to be positive. This wager may be wrong. It is possible that the real human costs of a distributed sovereignty system outweigh the benefits. I cannot prove otherwise. I can only tell you why I believe it.

I believe it because the current world — one of ubiquitous commercial surveillance, recommendation algorithms that amplify outrage to maximize engagement, health data sold to insurers without informed consent, communication metadata used to identify dissidents — this world has real, documented human costs. These costs are distributed unequally, hitting harder those who are already vulnerable: racialised minorities, political dissidents, victims of domestic violence whose abuser buys their location data, precarious workers whose digital habits influence their access to credit.

Yoshua Bengio warns us that technological tools can be turned against the most vulnerable. He is right. But this turning is already happening, massively, in the current centralized world. The question is not "how do we prevent tools from being turned against the vulnerable" — that perfect prevention does not exist. The question is: in what type of system do vulnerable people have the best tools to defend themselves, the best chances of recourse, and the least amount of power concentrated in the hands of actors who might wish them harm?
What remains unchanged after this act

After dwelling in these shadows throughout this act, here is what I have not changed my mind about. I still believe that families have the right to control their own digital space. I still believe that communities have the right to build their own trust networks. I still believe that the concentration of informational power in the hands of a few corporations or governments is a real threat to democracy and human dignity.

What I have changed, or rather, what this act has clarified: I now know that a distributed sovereignty system that does not take the protection of children seriously has betrayed its own founding principle. I know that community governance mechanisms must be designed with particular attention to the protection of minorities against the abuses of majorities. I know that the resilience of safeguards depends at least as much on the strength of democratic institutions as on the elegance of technical design.

And I know — I have always known, but this act has made it harder to ignore — that some people will use the freedom we are building to cause harm. Not by accident, not out of ignorance, but deliberately. This reality does not paralyse me. It compels me to be precise. To distinguish between real protective measures and theoretical ones. To admit the limits honestly rather than drowning them in visionary rhetoric.
The final question of this act

I have one more thing to say before closing this act. It is personal. This book bears my name — David Berthelotte. It is dedicated to Cynthia, to Jordane, and to Olivia. When I imagine the world this project could create, I imagine it for them. And when I imagine the shadows this act has explored, I imagine them for them too.

For Cynthia — if she ever found herself in a home where the digital walls became bars. For Jordane and Olivia — if someone ever used the sovereign spaces we are building to harm them. These thoughts did not lead me to abandon the project. They led me to design it differently. To demand that protections for the most vulnerable be architectural, not optional. To refuse compromises that sacrifice the safety of children on the altar of ideological consistency.

The shadows this act has explored are real. They will not disappear because we look away from them. They will not disappear because we look at them either. But looking at them honestly, naming them precisely, and designing systems that take them seriously — that is the only intellectual and moral stance I can defend. That is what I have tried to do here.

ONE FINAL SELF-CRITIQUE, THE HARDEST OF ALL: It is possible that I am wrong. Not about the technical details — about the fundamental direction. It is possible that a world of distributed digital sovereignty is, on average and in total, worse for the most vulnerable people than a world of well-regulated surveillance. I have no large-scale empirical evidence to the contrary. This project has not yet been deployed. The comparable historical experiments are too few and too different to permit reliable predictions. I believe the direction is right. But believing is not knowing, and I must say so aloud.
What this act does not resolve — and why that matters

There is a temptation, after listing safeguards and their limits, to conclude that the exercise is complete. That the risks have been "managed." This temptation is dangerous. The safeguards listed here are partial answers to problems that have no complete answers. No combination of technical measures will eliminate the risks described in this act. What these measures can do, at best, is reduce their probability, their impact, and the time before they are detected and corrected.

The real work that will transform the shadows into manageable risks is not technical — it is cultural, political, and institutional. It requires a society that understands digital technologies well enough to debate them democratically. It requires judicial institutions adapted to disputes born in distributed networks. It requires social workers trained to detect violence and exploitation in digitally sovereign environments. It requires a free and independent press that documents abuses when they occur. These conditions are as important as the technical architecture. And in many current contexts, they are more fragile.

I close this act with a conviction that has not changed since I began writing this book. The tools we build are morally neutral in the sense that they amplify what humans do — nothing more, nothing less. A saw cuts wood and human limbs. A knife prepares meals and injures people. The question is not to invent tools that cannot cause harm — that invention is impossible. The question is to build societies where institutions, culture, and accountability mechanisms minimize the conditions under which tools are turned against the most vulnerable. This book contributes to the technical part of that equation. The human part — the most important one — is yours.

What price are you willing to pay for sovereignty? And what price are you already paying, every day, for not having it? These two prices are not abstract. They are paid in freedom, in dignity, and sometimes in human lives. The decision of which price you prefer to pay is the most fundamental political decision this book asks you to consider. It cannot be delegated to engineers. It cannot be delegated to governments. It belongs to each one of you.

The interlude and the epilogue that follow will speak of building. Of how, concretely, an ordinary family in Laval or Sherbrooke or Rimouski begins the journey toward sovereignty — not as ideology, but as a series of small, concrete acts. But I could not have led you to those acts without first leading you here, into the shadows. Because building without having looked at the shadows is building in blindness. And blind constructions end up collapsing on the very people they were meant to protect.
